title: Hot day alert in south Bihar; temperature may touch 44°C: Intense sun, humidity to trouble residents despit... - Bhaskar English
full_text:
Hot day alert in south Bihar; temperature may touch 44°C:Intense sun, humidity to trouble residents despite no heatwave warning
The Meteorological Centre has issued a hot day alert for five districts of south Bihar on Saturday, with maximum temperatures likely to reach up to 44°C.
Officials said there is no heatwave warning, but people may face intense sunshine and humid heat, making conditions uncomfortable. In parts of north Bihar, the sky will remain partly cloudy, though no significant relief from heat is expected.
Rohtas hottest in 24 hours
In the past 24 hours, Rohtas was the hottest district, recording a maximum temperature of 44°C. In Patna, the maximum temperature stood at 40.6°C.
Streets empty as heat rises
The rising heat has affected daily life.
Roads remained largely deserted in Nalanda during the afternoon due to scorching sun.
In Jehanabad, schoolgirls were seen using umbrellas to shield themselves from the heat.
Some pictures related to the weather
April heat breaks recent trend
This April has seen a spike in temperatures compared to recent years. So far, the highest temperature of 44°C has been recorded in Dehri (Rohtas), among the highest in the past five years.
Weather systems behind the heat
Meteorologists said multiple weather systems are influencing conditions in Bihar:
- A low-pressure area has formed over Bihar, Jharkhand, and West Bengal at about 0.9 km altitude
- A cyclonic circulation over eastern Uttar Pradesh has merged with this system
- Strong westerly winds (80–90 knots) are blowing at around 12.6 km altitude
- These conditions are causing cloud formation in north Bihar and intense heat in south Bihar.
Patna may touch 41°C
In Patna, the weather is expected to remain clear with bright sunshine over the next 24 hours. The maximum temperature may reach 41°C, with hot westerly winds increasing discomfort. Heatwave-like conditions may develop during the afternoon.
Hot day vs heatwave
Hot day: When temperatures are above normal and conditions remain uncomfortable due to strong sun and heat.
Heatwave: Declared when temperatures reach 45°C or more, or remain above 40°C continuously, increasing the risk of heatstroke, dehydration, and exhaustion.
